name: Gates County

actions.title: One-Year Moratorium on Data Centers and High-Impact Data Processing Facilities
actions.summary: Gates County commissioners unanimously enacted a one-year moratorium barring acceptance or approval of any application for data centers, cryptocurrency mining facilities, or other high-impact data processing facilities, effective through December 17, 2026.

contextBlurb: Gates County was among North Carolina's earliest jurisdictions to enact a data center moratorium, acting proactively after receiving two inquiries from a firm that prepares ordinances to promote data center development and after the county's planning director heard developers were exploring projects in the state's northeastern corner. Officials cited particular concern about strain on the county's water system, which has itself been under a separate moratorium since 2018. All five speakers at the public hearing opposed data centers, citing impacts on wildlife, the electrical grid, water consumption, and noise.

name: Chatham County

actions.title: Twelve-Month Moratorium on Data Centers, Crypto Mining, and Associated Uses
actions.summary: The Chatham County Board of Commissioners approved a one-year moratorium on permitting of data centers, data processing facilities, and cryptocurrency mining operations in unincorporated areas, effective immediately through February 11, 2027 or until new zoning regulations are adopted, whichever comes first.

contextBlurb: Chatham County, situated west of Raleigh near the Research Triangle, enacted a moratorium following a public hearing that drew written and verbal comments from more than two dozen individuals. The county cited 'considerable amounts of water and energy usage' as necessitating the pause, which covers AI computing, cryptocurrency mining, web services, and genome sequencing. During the moratorium, planning staff will study environmental and community impacts and develop proposed zoning definitions and performance standards.

name: Town of Canton

actions.title: One-Year Moratorium on Data Centers, Crypto Mining, and High-Density Digital Infrastructure
actions.summary: Canton Town Council voted unanimously to impose a one-year pause on building any data centers, cryptocurrency mining operations, server farms, or other high-density digital infrastructure facilities after more than 150 residents attended a standing-room-only public hearing with 45 speakers in opposition.

contextBlurb: The Town of Canton in Haywood County passed its moratorium following intense community opposition triggered by reports that data center companies were eyeing the former Pactiv Evergreen paper mill site, a landmark shuttered in 2023 and demolished in early 2025. More than 150 residents attended the public hearing—exceeding the fire capacity of 49—with 45 individuals speaking, nearly all in opposition. During the moratorium, the town will study impacts and draft related zoning and land-use ordinances.

name: Stokes County

actions.title: Rezoning of ~1,845 Acres for Heavy Industrial Use (Project Delta)
actions.summary: The Stokes County Board of Commissioners voted 3-2 to rezone approximately 1,845 acres of residential-agricultural land along the Dan River for heavy industrial use, clearing the path for the 'Project Delta' hyperscale AI data center, overriding the planning board's recommendation to deny.

actions.title: Community Lawsuit to Void Project Delta Rezoning (Hairston Clan v. Stokes County)
actions.summary: Community groups and Walnut Cove residents, represented by SCSJ and SELC, filed suit in Stokes County Superior Court challenging the January 2026 rezoning as procedurally defective, alleging commissioners failed to provide required public notice, assess environmental impacts, or consider sacred cultural sites.

contextBlurb: Stokes County commissioners approved the controversial Project Delta rezoning by a narrow 3-2 vote in January 2026, opening more than 1,800 acres along the Dan River—and over a dozen other rural sites—to potential hyperscale data center development, despite the planning board's recommendation against it and fervent community opposition. Community opposition was so intense that an incumbent commissioner lost a primary challenge to a candidate who ran primarily on opposing the data center. A lawsuit filed in March 2026 by SCSJ and SELC asks the court to invalidate the rezoning, alleging violations of state procedural law and failure to consider noise, air quality, water, and culturally significant sites tied to the Saura people and Hairston family descendants.

name: Edgecombe County

actions.title: Unified Development Ordinance Amendment to Allow Data Centers in Industrial Zones
actions.summary: The Edgecombe County Board of Commissioners unanimously amended its Unified Development Ordinance to permit data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities within designated industrial zoning districts, enabling a proposed $19.2 billion, 900-MW data center campus at the Kingsboro Industrial Park.

actions.title: Land Sale Vote Postponed After Community Opposition
actions.summary: A vote to sell 120 acres of county-owned land at the Kingsboro Industrial Park to developer Energy Storage Solutions was pulled from the December 1, 2025 agenda following organized community opposition from Edgecombe Neighbors for Data Center Accountability.

contextBlurb: Edgecombe County amended its zoning ordinance in November 2025 to allow data centers in industrial districts, paving the way for a proposed $19.2 billion, 900-megawatt campus at the Kingsboro Industrial Park—a project that would rival the output of a nuclear reactor. Fierce community opposition led by Edgecombe Neighbors for Data Center Accountability delayed a land-sale vote, and anti-data-center sentiment ran so strong politically that a challenger focused almost entirely on the issue defeated a four-term incumbent county commissioner in the 2026 primary. Concerns center on water usage (up to 5 million gallons per day from the Tar River), potential air pollution from on-site natural gas and diesel generation, noise, and strain on utility rates.

name: Haywood County

actions.title: Canton (Municipal) Data Center Moratorium Within Haywood County
actions.summary: The Town of Canton, Haywood County's largest municipality, enacted a one-year moratorium on data centers following community opposition focused on the redevelopment of the former paper mill site, prompting broader county-level awareness of data center risks.

contextBlurb: Haywood County, in western North Carolina, became a focus of statewide data center debate when Canton's former Pactiv Evergreen paper mill—the town's largest employer for over a century before closing in 2023—attracted inquiries from data center developers after its demolition in early 2025. Canton's unanimous moratorium vote, driven by more than 150 residents cramming into a town hall with a fire capacity of only 49, drew statewide attention and was cited as a model by other communities. The broader Haywood County area continues to monitor whether additional data center interest will prompt county-level action.

name: Town of Apex

actions.title: Council Directs Staff to Draft One-Year Data Center Moratorium Ordinance
actions.summary: Apex Town Council voted unanimously to direct staff to draft a one-year moratorium on new data center permits, following public opposition that caused developer Natelli Investments to withdraw its application for a 300-MW 'New Hill Digital Campus' project.

contextBlurb: The Town of Apex in Wake County moved toward a moratorium in March 2026 after community opposition caused Maryland-based developer Natelli Investments to withdraw its application for a 300-megawatt data center on Shearon Harris Road—a project that would have generated an estimated $4 million for the town. Town leaders acknowledged that Apex has no existing rules about data centers in its ordinances, making the need for a regulatory pause clear. A formal moratorium ordinance was expected to come before the council for a vote as soon as April 2026.

name: Orange County

actions.title: Commissioners Vote to Schedule Public Hearing on One-Year Data Center Moratorium
actions.summary: Orange County commissioners voted to hold a public hearing in April 2026 before formally considering a one-year moratorium on large-scale data centers, with staff already directed to prepare land-use and zoning ordinance changes if the moratorium is approved.

contextBlurb: Orange County, home to Chapel Hill and bordering Durham, joined a growing list of North Carolina jurisdictions weighing a pause on data center development in March 2026. Commissioners scheduled an April public hearing on a potential one-year moratorium and simultaneously directed staff to begin preparing zoning ordinance amendments for when the moratorium period concludes. The action reflects a broader statewide wave of local resistance, with Orange County situated near the Research Triangle where data center developer interest has been particularly intense.
